title: Trinidadian curry duck
subtitle: Duck legs slow-braised in Trinidadian curry with green seasoning
excerpt: Duck legs braised low and long in a Trinidadian green-seasoned curry with cumin, thyme, and scotch bonnet until the meat falls from the bone. A special-occasion dish in the Indo-Trinidadian tradition.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian curry duck is a special-occasion dish in the Indo-Trinidadian community, prepared for religious celebrations and large gatherings. The duck is typically prepared in the same style as curry goat and curry chicken, using the green seasoning base and the 'burnt curry' technique. Duck legs are preferred for their fat content and flavour; the long braise renders the fat into the sauce and gives it depth. This recipe follows the Trinidadian style with coriander as a substitute for shadow beni.

content.text: Curry duck is richer and more complex than curry chicken: the fat from the duck renders into the sauce as it cooks and gives it a depth that no amount of additional liquid can replicate. The green seasoning marinade overnight is essential, duck is dense and needs more time than chicken for the flavours to penetrate. The long braise, at least 90 minutes, is what separates good curry duck from serviceable curry duck.

Skim the fat from the surface of the sauce before serving; there will be a significant amount of rendered duck fat. A day-ahead approach works very well: the fat solidifies overnight in the fridge and can be lifted off cleanly before reheating.

Blend the {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{coriander}} of coriander, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet with 2 tablespoons of water to a rough green paste. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Rub all over the {{duck-legs}} duck legs, working into any cuts in the skin. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Add the {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin and toast for 30 seconds. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 4 minutes. Add the duck legs skin-side down and brown for 5 minutes until the skin is golden. Turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Add the {{tomato}} tomatoes and 300 ml of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for 75 to 90 minutes until the duck meat is falling off the bone. Skim the surface fat before serving.

items.fix: Pat the duck legs completely dry before browning, even after the marinade. The moisture in the marinade prevents browning, remove the excess with kitchen paper.
items.cause: Too much moisture on the skin; pot not hot enough
items.symptom: Duck skin not crisping during browning

items.fix: Duck legs release a significant amount of fat. Skim with a spoon during cooking, or chill overnight and lift the solidified fat off the surface before reheating.
items.cause: Duck fat not skimmed; high-fat legs

items.fix: Duck legs vary considerably in size. Large legs can take 2 hours at a low simmer. Continue cooking until the meat pulls away from the bone at the knuckle with no resistance.
items.cause: Heat too low; legs very large
items.symptom: Meat not falling off the bone after 90 minutes

items.fix: The 30-second toast in the oil is non-negotiable for depth. An overnight marinade rather than 30 minutes makes a substantial difference with duck.
items.cause: Curry powder not properly toasted; marinade time too short

content.text: Curry duck is a fixture of the Indo-Trinidadian festive calendar. It appears at Divali and Eid alongside goat curry and chicken curry, cooked in large quantities for family gatherings. In the home kitchen it is a special dish, not everyday cooking. The cost and the time required put it in that category. Serve it with dhalpuri roti or paratha and white rice.
